briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for June 9, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis: Warning Signs for Domestic and International Marine Environments and Ecosystems
sections.content: Changes in the East Sea marine ecosystem are being observed, such as the recent catch of 170 bluefin tuna in Gangneung [1, 11, 31], which is analyzed as a direct impact of climate change. According to a UN report, the global sea level rise rate has more than doubled over the past decade, pushing oceans towards a tipping point [8, 123]. Warnings have also been issued that the rapid deterioration of ocean resilience due to climate change could lead to food security risks [9]. Domestically, the Seomjingang River Fish Ecology Center highlighted the biodiversity crisis by hosting a special exhibition titled 'Lives Disappearing Amidst the Climate Crisis' [33]. Internationally, the multifaceted impacts of the climate crisis are intensifying, with extreme weather increasing methane emissions from landfills [134] and fueling a surge in malaria in South Africa [145]. The UN Climate Change Executive Secretary emphasized that responding to the climate crisis is the most challenging and crucial task in human history, urging global vigilance [16, 136].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Efforts for Climate Change Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality Transition
sections.content: Domestically, multifaceted efforts are underway for climate change adaptation and the transition to a carbon-neutral society. KIOST plans to complete a marine science station at Wangdolcho in the East Sea to observe marine ecosystems and climate change in real-time [2, 17, 26]. The National Institute of Environmental Research is also strengthening its scientific response capabilities by hosting research competitions related to environmental satellites and climate change [3, 13, 23, 36]. Local governments are actively developing climate change adaptation measures, including strengthening infectious disease prevention networks [4, 15], implementing comprehensive flood and storm damage countermeasures [5], designating water circulation promotion zones [32], and fostering eco-friendly aquaculture [34]. Notably, Jangsu County has exceeded its greenhouse gas reduction target by 114%, receiving an 'excellent' rating for its climate crisis response performance [22, 61, 69], demonstrating tangible reduction efforts. Furthermore, the enforcement decree for a special act to strengthen the competitiveness of the steel industry and facilitate its carbon-neutral transition was approved by the Cabinet Meeting [55], and carbon-neutral efforts across industries, such as promoting wooden architecture and expanding low-carbon construction materials, are accelerating [40, 45, 46, 47, 77, 80, 84].

sections.title: ⚡️ Challenges of Renewable Energy Expansion and Energy Transition
sections.content: Positive news is emerging regarding the securing of domestic renewable energy generation assets. Bright Energy Partners (BEP) has surpassed 1 GW in solar power and Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) assets, securing a total of 1.3 GW of renewable energy generation assets [86, 91, 93, 98, 100, 102, 108, 111, 113, 118]. The completion of the Jeonnam Muan-Sinan transmission network is expected to alleviate renewable energy output curtailment [99, 101, 112]. Market interest is also growing, with new and renewable energy-related ETFs (Exchange Traded Funds) gaining prominence amidst surging oil prices [89, 105]. However, despite the government's call for accelerated renewable energy transition, there are criticisms that public enterprises are stagnating due to a lack of personnel [87, 97, 117]. The aviation industry has acknowledged that achieving carbon neutrality by 2050 will be challenging, as the supply of Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) remains at only 0.8% [48, 71]. Furthermore, a report stating that the world's largest banks provided $906 billion to the fossil fuel industry last year, an 'unfathomable' 8% increase from the previous year [147], highlights the paradoxical challenges of capital flow on the path to energy transition.

sections.title: 🌍 Enhancing Climate Awareness Through International Cooperation and Citizen Participation
sections.content: International cooperation and citizen participation in response to climate change are also being actively discussed. Domestically, participants are being recruited for the 2026 Local Conference of Youth (LCOY) on Climate Change [14]. Various local governments and educational institutions, including Sokcho City and the Nonsan-Gyeryong Office of Education, are spreading a culture of carbon-neutral practices among citizens, including future generations, through 'Visiting Carbon Neutral Schools' and Environmental Education Weeks [25, 43, 50, 51, 53, 54, 57, 58, 59, 60, 63, 64, 66, 67, 68, 70, 72, 73, 75, 76, 78, 82, 83, 106, 110, 120]. Internationally, discussions on shipbuilding and renewable energy cooperation between the Republic of Korea and the Faroe Islands of Denmark have taken place [96]. Gwangmyeong City in Gyeonggi Province presented its 'Gwangmyeong-type SDGs' sustainable development achievements to the international community, setting an example for local governance [81]. Meanwhile, diverse perspectives and discussions continue regarding the overall societal impact of climate change, including arguments that climate change is reshaping the meaning of nationhood and that Investor-State Dispute Settlement (ISDS) clauses threaten climate action [135], as well as questions raised by Christian leaders concerned about the climate crisis regarding donations [139, 148].

briefing.closing: As we have reviewed in today's briefing, the signs of the climate crisis are becoming increasingly clear. However, various efforts and innovations to overcome it are also continuing both domestically and internationally. Global solidarity and concrete actions are more crucial than ever to ensure these efforts bear fruit. This concludes today's climate briefing.
